  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "The section advances a thesis (LMCR), a gap statement (existing systems treat ambiguity as a downstream language problem), and a contribution claim (coupling grounding to stability analysis); review should verify these are internally coherent and accurately scoped.",
    "R1.2": "The introductory framing claims 'formal Lyapunov stability guarantees' but the switching layer is operationalized as confidence-thresholding with no Lyapunov function, invariance proof, or region-of-attraction derivation presented; this mismatch between stated formalism and described mechanism is a load-bearing logical inconsistency.",
    "R1.3": "The 'formal Lyapunov stability guarantees' claim is entirely unsupported by any proof, theorem, or citation; the experimental figures (31%→12% failure, 3.1→4.2 Likert) are asserted without confidence intervals, sample-size justification, or inter-rater reliability data; evidence-claim alignment is load-bearing.",
    "R1.4": "The section claims novelty in explicitly coupling the language-grounding layer to closed-loop stability analysis but cites no prior work to establish the delta; without grounding the novelty claim against the literature, the contribution assessment is incomplete.",
    "R1.6": "Writing is generally fluent but contains technically imprecise phrases ('certifiable region of attraction of the plant' without definition, 'practical recipe') that a review should flag; writing quality is a central expected responsibility.",
    "R1.7": "The section contains zero citations despite asserting properties of 'existing systems,' invoking Lyapunov theory, and referencing a 7-DoF manipulator platform; citation coverage and attribution are clearly expected in any substantive review of a technical paper section."

  "clarification_question": "Should the review focus primarily on technical and logical soundness—e.g., whether the Lyapunov stability claim is justified by the described confidence-thresholding mechanism—or primarily on writing quality and clarity, with technical correctness treated as out of scope?"
}

rationale.R1.1: The section advances a thesis (LMCR), a gap statement (existing systems treat ambiguity as a downstream language problem), and a contribution claim (coupling grounding to stability analysis); review should verify these are internally coherent and accurately scoped.
rationale.R1.2: The introductory framing claims 'formal Lyapunov stability guarantees' but the switching layer is operationalized as confidence-thresholding with no Lyapunov function, invariance proof, or region-of-attraction derivation presented; this mismatch between stated formalism and described mechanism is a load-bearing logical inconsistency.
rationale.R1.3: The 'formal Lyapunov stability guarantees' claim is entirely unsupported by any proof, theorem, or citation; the experimental figures (31%→12% failure, 3.1→4.2 Likert) are asserted without confidence intervals, sample-size justification, or inter-rater reliability data; evidence-claim alignment is load-bearing.
rationale.R1.4: The section claims novelty in explicitly coupling the language-grounding layer to closed-loop stability analysis but cites no prior work to establish the delta; without grounding the novelty claim against the literature, the contribution assessment is incomplete.
rationale.R1.6: Writing is generally fluent but contains technically imprecise phrases ('certifiable region of attraction of the plant' without definition, 'practical recipe') that a review should flag; writing quality is a central expected responsibility.
rationale.R1.7: The section contains zero citations despite asserting properties of 'existing systems,' invoking Lyapunov theory, and referencing a 7-DoF manipulator platform; citation coverage and attribution are clearly expected in any substantive review of a technical paper section.

parsed.clarification_question: Should the review focus primarily on technical and logical soundness—e.g., whether the Lyapunov stability claim is justified by the described confidence-thresholding mechanism—or primarily on writing quality and clarity, with technical correctness treated as out of scope?
